sentences.text: Yes.
I've been there.
Yes and no.
For example, the Polish students-- [NON-ENGLISH] what-- association-- they had been very, very nationalistic, and of course against Germany.
But at the same time, they've been very much against Jews and partly England.
Well, it's took place after the start of the war.
The Germans had declared war on Poland and even before they came-- before the news came over the radio that the war was on.
We had, already, airplanes bombing us before the official declaration.
The 1st of September, '39.
Yes, and at the same time the German had declared war over the air-- over the radio-- that was the first day.
And the next day or two, I -- I don't remember exactly anymore-- then England came in.
And that's when the demonstration took place.
On the 1st of September, we had been back in Warsaw from a summers-- uh, what do you call it?
Summer house or-- from holiday, summer holidays.
I'd been coming back from college because I'd been doing certification in college.
And Vera and her husband and the children and the grandmother they've been in a suburb of Warsaw in a little villa.
Well, we all came back into Warsaw.
First of all, Vera's husband got called up, mobilized.
He was a Polish army officer.
Israel.
Israel Furst.
And he was an officer of the Polish army, so of course he got called up.
But he provided for us quite a bit of food.
A bag of rice, a bag of flour, a bag of this and that.
And not very far from us, around the corner, there was a very big hospital.
And after the first few bombings, that hospital got bombed.
And they moved over into the Jewish hostel-- the students' hostel-- because that was also a very good and big building.
And we had to go.
We had to leave.
Not very many.
No not very many.
Because it to was at the very end of the school holidays.
There had been very, very few elderly people who sort of lived there.
We went into Warsaw proper, to a friend's place.
This lady friend, she went to her mother and left us her flat.
Yes.
That was [NON-ENGLISH] street, [NON-ENGLISH] 20, I think it was.
And then the siege-- a few days later because the Germans came into Poland, like into battle.
It was a siege of Warsaw.
You know that Warsaw has resisted for six weeks.
And during those six weeks there had been constant bombings.
I'd been in the air defense, and under the roof, when the bomb came down.
And the stairs fell down, and I fell up.
Anyways, the family was on the first floor, and somehow or another I slipped down.

But nobody was anymore in the place which used to be the first floor, and I went into the street and somehow or another I met the rest-- Vera, my older sister, the two twins, and Vera's daughter Nina-- she was only a baby-- a few houses away in the same state.
But we missed our grandmother.
She must have run into a different direction.
And she could hardly speak Polish.
She spoke more German than Polish, and at that time to be a German in Poland was not the nicest thing.
Anyway, after a few days we found her and brought her back.
A few houses from the house which got the bombed until the end of the siege.
And then we looked for another accommodation.
Vera's husband was away.
He was at the war.
There was no water.
There was no electricity.
There was no food.
There would be queues for bread and-- but they started to restore things slowly.
And we went into a flat in another street in Warsaw.
[NON-ENGLISH] it was called.
And after a few weeks Vera's husband came back.
He had the opportunity to go across the Romanian border and into the Anders' Army into-- so Romania into Hungary into Italy.
But knowing that he had a family with little children and an old woman, he left and came back to Warsaw to look after his family.
Oh, that was much later.
You see, we've been talking about '39.
The war and the start to the war, then the siege of Warsaw.
That was all '39.
And the ghetto was not till '40, and then '41 it was closed.
Well, we started to work, of course.

Well, Israel, or as we called him [NON-ENGLISH], he was a manager of the Jewish hostel and the board of this hostel had a president who was the elder and counselor in the Jewish community-- or Jewish Council, Judenrat, he called him up to the Judenrat and he was made-- he took over the economics department.
And I started to work in the Jewish quarantine hospital.
First of all, it was Adam Czerniakow.
Which you know.
And you see we brought from college, from my parents' home, very valuable furniture.
And when we had to leave the hostel, where the furniture had been, my parents furniture was taken over to Warsaw.

The [NON-ENGLISH] sold part of this furniture to the Judenrat because it was elegant, and the Judenrat needed at least one good room where they could meet the representatives from the Germans who called on the Judenrat quite often.
Anyway, that helped us too, financially.
It was the council.
It was the same that was before the war.
Don't forget that Warsaw had a very, very large Jewish population before the wall.
And thus the Jewish Council was a very active, and very helpful to the community organization.
And there had been the president, which was Adam Czerniakow.
There was the vice president, which was Mark Lichtenbaum and then there had been councilors, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] and this other one I remember, [NON-ENGLISH].
There had been some others as well.
That was the board.
I think there had been up to eight or 10.
I don't recall exactly.
Well, one day they have it decreed that they need to have a count of all the Jews and in 48 hours all Jewish people living in Warsaw had to be registered.
So you can imagine what it was like.
It was work, 48 hours without pause.
And everybody had to come to the council and get registered.
Yes, I did.
And I slept for a couple of hours on one of the desks, because it was--
No, not yet.
No.
But it came very soon.
You had to wear a white arm band with a blue Star of David.
What do you mean?
Yes.
In your home, in your house, you could do whatever you wanted.
Of course there was.
It was quite evident that the ghetto was closed, not only formed but closed.
Yes.
Yes, yes, it was quite evident that it was going to happen.
From what point of view?
Well, it was announced through the press and through the radio.
Every Jew had to move into a given part, a few streets.
That was-- was it.
Well, a lot-- I would say most of the Warsaw Jews lived in this area which became the ghetto.
There hadn't been very many, like we did, who lived in the Polish side, so to say, among Poles.
But the Jewish hostel, the student hostel, that was in Praga, on the other side of the river Vistula.
And that was a very Polish part.
And we lived all the time in the Polish part.
Well, we all moved into the ghetto.
You had to.
You had no choice once you've been registered as being a Jew.
First we lived in a little flat in [NON-ENGLISH].
And then we got a better one, bigger one, later on.
And we always had some-- some flat or another.
Very crowded.
Half a million people had been crowded into a few streets.
First, there was a larger ghetto, it was so-called the small and the big ghetto.
And then when they cut off the small ghetto, in which the original Judenrat, the Jewish Council, was in the [NON-ENGLISH] street.
But when they cut this off the Council had to shift.
There was not really any difference other than streets.
Some streets, including the one in which the Council stood, had been taken out of there-- declared not to be a ghetto.
The Jews had to get out of there.
And move further north to the bigger ghetto.
That must have been end of '40 or beginning of '41, something like that.
More crowded, more crowded.
Once it was closed, there was none.
Once they closed the ghetto there had been gates which had been guarded by German SS people, men, and you couldn't get in or out.
Was some time.
Yes, in the initial period, it was possible.
Yes, as a matter of fact, some of my friends had been working for them, for the Judenrat.
Until it was closed.
Non-Jewish people.
That depends.
There had been different reactions from different people.
There had been-- there had been the people who had been friendly and tried to help, and that was the minority.
The majority was very happy that the Jews were taken out of the larger city and closed away.
Yes
Well, the typhus was rife amongst the Jewish population because of the terrific crowding, and those people who survived they still had been carriers.
Therefore, they couldn't go back into their homes.
They had to come to the quarantine hospital and stay there and recuperate.
At least they got some food and proper care for a couple of weeks at least.
And then they'd be discharged and could go back to their homes.
And this quarantine was in a former Polish gymnasium school.
It was a beautiful library.
[NON-ENGLISH] street.
Very close to the ghetto.
40, 50, around this much.
Sometimes more, sometimes less.
The Jewish Council had-- called Hospital and Medical Department.
It was Dr.
Fliederbaum who was well known to the community.
Because before the war Warsaw had a very good Jewish hospital in a part which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was very eminent.
And there was-- there had been other doctors, as well, who came now and again to see what was going on.
Judenrat, the council-- Jewish Council.
Well, they had a budget.
The Economic Department had budgeting for every other department.
The same as every other council in the world-- you had to pay some, call it rates or call it whatever you want.
Of course.
Not only for the hospital.
They had been raided.
From the government, from the Poles, not that I know of.
There could have been.
I don't exclude it.
But not that I remember or know of.
When the ghetto was closed.
I think it was, but I don't recall the date.
No.
Not anymore.
Well, when the quarantine hospital was outside the ghetto, then it wasn't too bad because there was still some movement between the Irish, Polish, and the ghetto side.
Then it wasn't too bad conditions.
But once it was closed that's when it really became very, very difficult.
The water was city supplied.
Electricity was city supplied.
The telephones had been still working.
And the food was allocated just the same as to the Polish people.
You had cards for all sorts of things.
And apart from this, smuggling.
Ration cards and some things had been sold in shops as well.
By the council.
Oh, I remember a funny thing.
One Jewish family had a young child, and they kept a goat in their flat for the milk of the child, and they registered this as Mrs.
Goat.
And they got food cards for Mrs.
Goat.
Oh, yes, of course.
Everybody had to do something to live.
Well, there-- you know, Jews are very enterprising.
They had their own enterprises.
They had all sort of shops and bakeries and this sort of thing.
And the Council had employed a big number of people.
There had been work squads going out from the ghetto onto the Polish side, especially for building for the Germans.
Yes.
That was later.
That was in the later stages.
That was already after Czerniakow's death.
You know that Adam Czerniakow, he had committed suicide after getting to know what the plans had been.
I got a job in the building department.
They did all sorts of building jobs in and out of the ghetto.
Out of the ghetto it was for the Germans.
Inside it was ordered by the German Reich.
And not-- or this too.
For example, they built a beautiful kindergarten, a place with playground equipment.
And then they built a bridge across the Polish side of the street.
All sorts of jobs.
They had been architects, and they had been-- what do you call the engineers who build the roads and bridges?
Pardon?
Yes, and of course I'd been typing all this-- you know, the material necessary.
And because I was bilingual, when they had to present it to the Germans-- the plans plus the materials-- I had plenty of work to do.
What did I do?
No, I didn't present the plan.
I typed it.
I'm the typing girl.
There had been two people going to the Germans to present it and sometimes the Germans came to the Judenrat.
It was engineer, Lichtenbaum yeah, son of the president.
He was an engineer.
And then you may have heard about engineer, [NON-ENGLISH].
Probably heard the name [NON-ENGLISH].
Did you also hear about the two painters [NON-ENGLISH]?
No?
That's another story.
Anyway, it was usually the young Lichtenbaum who went to the Germans to present the buildings.
Building plans and the types-- what do you call this?
I can't remember in English.
When you type out all the necessary timber and all the materials for the building?
Like a tin-- no, no not a tinder.
Not a tinder--
It's a special name for it.
The Jewish police force.
Headed by Szerynski who was before the war very high in the Polish police.
As a matter of fact, he was in the French Legion.
You know what the French Legion was?
And he also committed suicide once he got to know what awaits and what was asked from him.
To-- to the Judenrat and through the Judenrat to the Germans.
And maybe directly, I can't really tell you exactly.
Because that was out of my reach.
I haven't been the right to know all the details of this relationship between.
SS men.
Well, I had a pass and the permission to go into the Polish side without the armband, and I made good use of it.
I can assure you.
Because they'd been embarrassed, the Germans, that a Jew came to their buildings.
That was embarrassing for them.
Yes.
Pardon?
Of course, they knew I was Jewish.
But they'd been embarrassed to have a Jewish person coming to their buildings.
Well, all sorts of things-- different things to different people.
We started to plan of course.
To live as Poles on the Polish side.
Because it was evident that the ghetto would not last forever.
And because I had the permission to move without the armband and because I was bilingual in German-- although I rode in the German part of the Polish tramways.
And I hired a flat on the Polish side, and we brought out the grandma and the kids, Roman and Eva.
I don't remember quite.
But the children, Roman and Eva, they'd been taken-- and Vera-- they went all together to Otwock, which was a Jewish mental hospital.
Which was fairly big.
And during the war it got smaller and part off it was turned into a resort, Jewish resort.
Otwock is-- I wouldn't say a suburb-- it was a line, a railway line from Warsaw towards the northeast.
It was a line which was to later used for going to Treblinka.
I think so, it was.
Anyway, Vera and all the kids, Vera and Nina, Roman and Eva, they went to the sanatorium, it was called, in Otwock.
It was Jewish.
That was also a ghetto.
No, it was not part of the Polish ghetto, but it was the Otwock ghetto.
You could, you could go.
They went by car.
Anyway, they went to Otwock.
And Ida, the grandmother, she was smuggled out of the ghetto with some furniture on a wagon, horse-drawn wagon with some furniture, which was supposed to go for the Germans in a building.
We put her and she was said to be the cleaning lady for when the furniture arrived in the new building.
But I'd been waiting already for this wagon in a side street of Warsaw with another horse-drawn wagon and have transferred her from the Jewish one to the Polish one.
And taken Ida, together with the furniture, into our new flat.
And then when Otwock was nearly finished, we got the new that it would be.
And we took them out.
I provided for them a little house two stations away from Otwock in [NON-ENGLISH].
And the children with Ida stayed there, and Vera, with Nina, went back to the ghetto, to her husband.
Well, as I told you, they've been-- we've been divided.
Ida and the twins they're in [NON-ENGLISH].
Vera and Nina went back to the ghetto to her husband.
And I've been floating everywhere.
